nodes.name: Geometry
nodes.description: Geometric formulas, shapes, and spatial concepts

items.prompt: What measurement of a circle does the formula πr² give?
items.explanation:
Archimedes proved it c. 250 BCE using the method of exhaustion
Derives from integral calculus (∫₀ʳ 2πr dr)
Doubling the radius quadruples the area
Basis for computing areas of ellipses (πab)
Appears on virtually every standardized math test

items.prompt: What measurement does the formula 4/3 πr³ give?
items.explanation:
Archimedes discovered it and considered it his greatest achievement
Sphere volume = 2/3 of the circumscribed cylinder
His tombstone reportedly bore a sphere inscribed in a cylinder
Cavalieri's principle provides an elegant proof
Related to integration: rotating a semicircle around its diameter

items.prompt: What is the name for the perimeter of a circle, given by 2πr or πd?
items.explanation:
From Latin circumferre ("to carry around")
Ratio of circumference to diameter defines π
Eratosthenes used Earth's circumference shadow measurements c. 240 BCE, getting remarkably close to the true value
The wheel's odometer principle measures distance by counting circumference rotations
Any circle's circumference divided by its diameter yields the same constant (π)

items.prompt: What measurement does the formula 4πr² give?
items.explanation:
Exactly four times the area of a great circle (πr²)
Archimedes proved this too
Useful for calculating planetary surfaces and balloon expansion
Doubling the radius quadruples the surface area (same scaling as a circle's area)
Key formula in physics for radiation, gravity, and electrostatic flux

items.prompt: What is the collective name for the five regular convex polyhedra: tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, and icosahedron?
items.explanation:
Tetrahedron (4 triangles), cube (6 squares), octahedron (8 triangles), dodecahedron (12 pentagons), icosahedron (20 triangles)
Named after Plato, who associated each with a classical element in *Timaeus* (c. 360 BCE): fire, earth, air, universe, water
Euclid proved in the *Elements* that exactly five exist
Kepler tried to fit planetary orbits between nested Platonic solids in *Mysterium Cosmographicum* (1596)
The proof that only five exist relies on the constraint that face angles meeting at a vertex must sum to less than 360°

items.prompt: What formula states that for any convex polyhedron, V − E + F = 2 (vertices minus edges plus faces equals 2)?
items.explanation:
For any convex polyhedron: vertices minus edges plus faces equals 2
Published by Euler in 1758
A cube: 8 − 12 + 6 = 2; a tetrahedron: 4 − 6 + 4 = 2
Foundation of topology — generalized to the Euler characteristic χ for any surface; for a torus, χ = 0 instead of 2
Descartes discovered a related result c. 1630 but never published it

items.prompt: What conic section, described by y = ax² + bx + c, is formed by slicing a cone parallel to its slant and has a single focus and directrix?
items.explanation:
Conic section produced when a plane intersects a cone parallel to its side
Every point is equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix)
Projectile trajectories follow parabolic paths (ignoring air resistance) — Galileo proved this in 1638
Parabolic mirrors focus parallel light rays to a single point (used in satellite dishes, headlights, telescopes)
Named by Apollonius of Perga (c. 200 BCE)
The reflective property makes parabolic antennas the standard shape for radio telescopes

items.prompt: What conic section has two foci, where the sum of distances from any point on the curve to both foci is constant?
items.explanation:
A circle is a special case where both foci coincide
Kepler's first law (1609): planetary orbits are ellipses with the Sun at one focus
Eccentricity (0 to 1) measures how elongated: 0 = circle, close to 1 = very elongated
Earth's orbit has eccentricity ~0.017 (nearly circular)
The "whispering gallery" effect in elliptical rooms works because sound from one focus reflects to the other
Apollonius of Perga named it (meaning "deficiency" in Greek)

items.prompt: What conic section has two branches, where the difference of distances from any point to the two foci is constant?
items.explanation:
Formed when a plane intersects both halves of a double cone
Has two asymptotes that the curve approaches but never touches
The function y = 1/x is a rectangular hyperbola
Used in LORAN navigation: ships determined position from the time difference of radio signals from two stations
Cooling tower shapes are hyperbolic — the geometry provides structural strength
Named by Apollonius of Perga (meaning "excess" in Greek)

items.prompt: What surface has only one side and one edge, created by giving a strip of paper a half-twist and joining the ends?
items.explanation:
Discovered independently by August Ferdinand Möbius and Johann Benedict Listing in 1858
Drawing a line down the center returns to the starting point after traversing both "sides" — proving it has only one surface
Cutting one down the middle produces not two Möbius strips but one longer twisted loop
The recycling symbol (♻) is a Möbius strip
Used in conveyor belts (even wear on both "sides") and continuous-loop recording tapes
The simplest non-orientable surface in topology

items.prompt: What is the name for a pattern of shapes that covers a plane with no gaps or overlaps?
items.explanation:
From Latin tessella (small square tile)
Only three regular polygons tessellate alone: equilateral triangles, squares, and regular hexagons
M.C. Escher made tessellations famous through his art — interlocking birds, fish, and lizards
Islamic geometric art extensively uses tessellation patterns since representational art is restricted
In 2023, a hobbyist discovered the first "einstein" tile (a single shape that tiles aperiodically) — the "hat" shape
Penrose tilings (1974) use two shapes to tile aperiodically, related to quasicrystals

items.prompt: What type of geometric pattern is self-similar at every scale, meaning the whole has the same structure as its parts?
items.explanation:
Term coined by Benoît Mandelbrot in 1975 from the Latin fractus ("broken")
The Mandelbrot set is the most famous example — infinitely complex at every magnification
Can have non-integer dimensions: the Koch snowflake has dimension ~1.26
Found throughout nature: coastlines, fern leaves, blood vessels, Romanesco broccoli
Mandelbrot's "How Long Is the Coast of Britain?" (1967) showed coastline length depends on measurement scale
Used in computer graphics to generate realistic landscapes, clouds, and textures

items.prompt: What non-orientable surface has no boundary and cannot be embedded in three-dimensional space without self-intersection?
items.explanation:
Named after Felix Klein, who described it in 1882
Like a Möbius strip but closed — no boundary and no distinction between inside and outside
Cannot exist in 3D without passing through itself; needs 4 dimensions to exist without self-intersection
Can be constructed by gluing two Möbius strips together along their edges
The name may be a mistranslation from German Kleinsche Fläche ("Klein surface") to Klein bottle
Physical glass models are sold as mathematical curiosities and desk ornaments

items.prompt: What rectangle has sides in the ratio 1:φ (≈1:1.618) and can be subdivided into a square and a smaller similar rectangle infinitely?
items.explanation:
Side ratio equals the golden ratio φ ≈ 1.618
Remove a square from one end and the remaining rectangle is another golden rectangle — this process repeats infinitely
Connecting the corners of successive squares creates a golden spiral (approximating a logarithmic spiral)
Claimed to appear in the Parthenon's facade, though measurements are debated
The Fibonacci spiral approximates the golden spiral as Fibonacci numbers increase
Salvador Dalí used golden rectangles explicitly in *The Sacrament of the Last Supper* (1955)

items.prompt: What family of geometries rejects Euclid's parallel postulate, allowing parallel lines to converge or diverge?
items.explanation:
Euclidean geometry assumes exactly one line through a point parallel to a given line — non-Euclidean geometries relax this
Hyperbolic geometry (Lobachevsky, Bolyai, 1830s): infinitely many parallel lines; triangle angles sum to less than 180°
Spherical/elliptic geometry (Riemann, 1854): no parallel lines exist; triangle angles sum to more than 180°
Einstein's general relativity (1915) models gravity as curved spacetime — non-Euclidean geometry describes reality at cosmic scales
Gauss reportedly measured angles of a large triangle between mountain peaks to test for curvature (results inconclusive)
For 2,000 years, mathematicians tried to prove the parallel postulate from Euclid's other axioms; its independence was the breakthrough
